sentences.text: My name is Michael Honey.
Michael, M-I-C-H-A-E-L.
Honey, H-O-N-E-Y.
Formerly Honigwachs, H-O-N-I-G-W-A-C-H-S.
I had a nickname as a very small child, before I went to school, which my brother, [?
Milos, ?] gave me, and that was Puppe.
Puppe means dolly.
I was apparently quite a beautiful child, hence the name.
The funny thing is that because I was a boy, or I am a boy, [LAUGHS] in German, they called me derr Puppe, derr being the personal pronoun, masculine.
But in fact, the correct German says die Puppe, not derr Puppe.
So whenever I was introduced, in German, to people and people said, this is derr Puppe, everybody started laughing.

When I went to my first class at elementary school, at age six, and we were asked what our nicknames were and I said my nickname was Puppe, the teacher, who was a very lovely man, said, oh, you can't go through life with a name like that.
And he said, to me, well, you look like a little bear, so I will call you Misha, which is the Czech sort of diminutive of a bear.
My date of birth is the 3rd of September, 1929.
I was born in Novy Jicin, N-O-V-Y, separate word Jicin, J-I-C with a hook I-- long I-- N.
Which is in Moravia in Czechoslovakia, or what used to be Czechoslovakia, now the Czech Republic.
But it's, in fact, in the part of Czechoslovakia which, in prewar days, was called the Sudetenland.
My father's name is Salomon.
My mother's name is Adele Cirla.
That's on her birth certificate.
Her Hebrew name is [?
Tsila, ?] and in her letters to us at various times that she wrote, she always signed herself [?
Tsila. ?]
Oh no, I've got much earlier memories than that.

We went to Poland quite often, because my father was imported-- importing rabbit skin furs for the industry in my hometown, which was the manufacturer of felt hats, and felt is made up of the pile of rabbit skins.
And in the 25 years that we lived in Novy Jicin, my father more or less supplied all the three factories with rabbit skin.
So we-- we traveled to Poland quite often in prewar years.
I think I've been there about three or four times as a child.
One particular vivid memory is around 1933, perhaps 1934.
It's really quite early in my life.
I remember that both my brother, [?

Milos, ?] and I were bought new blue overcoats and black Wellingtons-- shiny, new black Wellingtons-- because we went, actually, in the winter to a town called Zakopane in the mountains, and we spent the week in Zakopane.
And my father was buying rabbit skins on the telephone.
The situation-- when I asked my father about this early memory after the war, I said, why were you on the telephone all the time?
And-- or why didn't you go to Warsaw and to the various places where rabbit skins were being sold?
So he said, well, actually, I wasn't telling people that I was in Poland because as soon as I came to Poland, the price rose.
And what's a memory that you have of your mother at an early age?
That particular time was important, and I'm not sure whether it was the visit in 1933 or the visit in 1934.
During the time that we were in Krakow with my aunt and my uncle-- that was my father's youngest sister, [?
Pepcha. ?] My uncle's name was Lvov Besen.
He was a lawyer, an out of work lawyer, incidentally, but we stayed with them.
And my mother disappeared for about a week.
And the reason that she disappeared is because her half-brother died in Tarnobrzeg.
Tarnobrzeg is the city where she was born and where my grandfather on my father's side was also born there.
And my great-grandfather nearly became rabbi of Tarnobrzeg in 1821.
So my uncle, [?
Hersh ?] [?
Malach ?] died in 1934, and she went to sit shiva in Tarnobrzeg.
And that was rather strange because father was seeing to his business.
He had lots of friends in Krakow because this was his hometown.
He was born in Krakow.
And he met people in the street, and there were all kinds of meetings that took place.
Also family, other sisters and brothers, and his youngest brother, Joseph, died about six months earlier.
He must have died quite young, perhaps in his late 30s.
And we made a visit to an uncle of my father's whose name was Dov Behr Honigwachs, and he had a store with secondhand furniture in the Jewish district of Krakow.
And we walked there, which was a long way to walk, and I got quite tired.
But it was a fascinating place.
The shop was sort of on a corner, a very acute corner.
There was a lane on one side, going slightly downhill, and there was this shop with all kinds of bric-a-brac and secondhand furniture.

And we-- we-- we walked in and father wanted to know whether there was any money left, because his uncle, Dov Behr cleared the flat where my grandfather and Josef lived, which actually belonged to my father.
My father somehow sold it earlier.
But we just visited this uncle to see whether the furniture that was in the flat fetched any money, and from what I remember, Dov Behr was rather brusque with my father.
It's the first time that I saw someone talk somewhat disrespectfully to my father.
I mean, it was his uncle, you see, so he was much, much senior to my father, who was then in his early 50s, I suppose.
And my father was an imposing man.
I mean, he-- the people weren't rude to him because he was-- you know, he was [NON-ENGLISH], he was a-- you could look at him and see that he was a man of importance.
But this was the first time that I've ever seen anyone talk to-- talk down to my father.
And my uncle sort of said, there's no money left.
And I had to pay the cleaners because the flat was terribly dirty when Josef died, and in fact, I made a loss on it.
And I didn't quite understand the conversation.
The conversation, obviously, took place in Yiddish.
I knew Yiddish.
It was like a third language.
I'll tell you later why.
And when we walked out of the shop, we walked down this lane, downhill, because Father had some other errand to-- to go and visit somebody, and I-- and he was holding my hand.
And I asked Father, why his uncle, was so rude to him.
And [LAUGHS] I remember it so distinctly.
Father said, oh, he thinks his ancestor was a greater rabbi than mine.
[LAUGHTER]
Well, quite a lot in some ways but very little in other ways.
Novy Jicin, as I said, was a German town.
The Jewish community was also oriented towards German culture, because it was Austria-Hungary not so many years ago.
I mean, my father-- my parents only settled in Novy Jicin because father was in the Austrian army in the barracks in Novy Jicin.
And when he got [INAUDIBLE], he stayed in the town.
My father, for example, was [NON-ENGLISH] of the community, [NON-ENGLISH].
So since the death of the rabbi in 1924-- that was Rabbi Dr.
Mandl-- my father sort of ran the services together with the [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH] actually took the services, but my father was senior warden.
Well, there was only one warden.
It was a small community.
And my father understood the whole process of the year's conduct of services-- holidays, prayers-- he-- he knew what should be done, so there was no necessity of a rabbi.
Well, I remember Purim services when-- I still remember Purim services when the widow, Frau Dr.
Mandl, as we called her-- I-- I still remember when she used to take us in cheder.
I was about four or five.
She was supposed to teach us Hebrew but she never-- she never actually managed.
And the-- the-- the thing was that this was the German Reform Synagogue.
My father-- my father's background wasn't German Reform, but he adapted himself to German Reform.
I mean, I remember, as a child, walking on Shabbat with my parents from services and almost every Saturday, my mother would nag my father about the organ in the synagogue.
There was a beautiful organ which actually we-- it had-- it had pedals, so you had to pump it.
And a Goy had to come and play the organ because on Saturday, it's not allowed for a Jew to play.

And then another Goy would have to come and pump the organ because, you know, electrically driven organs, in those days, were still-- perhaps they were still-- already in existence, but it was-- this was-- this was one of the old, conventional organs where you had to sit on a stool, and you had to pump with your feet.
Of course, all the kids wanted to pump the organ, and to my mother, this was [YIDDISH] Shabbat, you know?
And the organ was on the balcony where the women sat.
So she would chase us away from pumping the organ.
And you could then hear that the organ gave up, [LAUGHS] so some other kid had to step in.
And she got annoyed again.
She wanted the organ done away with, and this was a conversation on the way home every Shabbat.
And father always said, look, I'm not that important in this community.

And if I tell them not to play the organ, and to do away with the organ altogether from the synagogue, they will call me a [INAUDIBLE], you know, because this is reform and we play the organ with the services.
Just as a sideline, in 1992, when we had the-- we had a-- a get together of Jews from [INAUDIBLE], which my brother, [?
Shragga, ?] organized in 1992.
Unfortunately, he couldn't make it at the time because he had a heart bypass operation and the wound on his leg didn't heal.
So just when the commemoration days were taking place, he couldn't come and I had to deputize for him to-- to sort of give the speeches and-- and talk.
And when we got into the synagogue, the synagogue is now used as the town archive and the chief archivist is a Dr.
Hubbolt, ?] a Czech, quite a nice man, historian.
The first question I asked him, what have you done with the organ?
[LAUGHTER] Of course, the organ is gone because the synagogue was devastated in 1938 during the Kristallnacht.
Well, that's an interesting question because we were six boys.
I'm the youngest of six boys--
Yes, the eldest was Leo.
He-- he became Dr.
Leo Honigwachs in 1938.
He went to the University of Brno.
Now he went to German schools, German gymnasium, and his mother tongue was German because the town was a German Sudetenland town.
And in 1918, when Czechoslovakia became Czechoslovakia, there wasn't a single Czech school in the town, not even an elementary school.
So he went entirely through German schools, and then when he went to university, he had to swap over from German to Czech.
And in fact, his first two years at university were very difficult because his professors used to say to him, you know, your essays are unacceptable.
If-- if you want to be a doctor, the first thing you have to learn is how to speak properly because he didn't know Czech.
So that was rather difficult.
The second one was Henry, Hermann in German or Heinrich.
His Hebrew name was [INAUDIBLE].
He also went to German schools-- German Gymnasium-- and he then went to a German speaking trade school.
And he went into my father's business and his mother tongue was German.
He spoke some Czech, very haltingly.
Now the third one is [INAUDIBLE] or [?
Shragga, ?] in Hebrew.
Ferdinand or Fritz, we used to call him, but in the Jewish Youth Movement, we called him [?
Alif, ?] so multiplicity of names because of different languages.
But he went to elementary school in German.
The first three years, he went to a German gymnasium.
And the last four years, he went to the Czech gymnasium.
Now he spoke perfect Czech because of his change at the age, about 12 or 13.
And [?
Mortre, ?] Mordecai, or Max, Maximilian, he went to German elementary school and swapped over to German gymnasium.
He left on Youth [?
Alliah ?] in 1937, so he spoke perfect Czech because he had six years of gymnasium.
He didn't actually finish [NON-ENGLISH] because my father took the view that he had to begin liquidating opposition in Novy Jicin and [?
Mortre ?] was the least problematical.
Milos ?] and-- well, I haven't come to the next two but there was a next brother, next to me, and myself.
We were still small children, so we couldn't be sent away.
Max was the first, really sort of grownup boy-- not grown up but well adolescent-- he was tall and-- and self-assured.
And [?
Shragga ?] was involved in studies at a-- at a textile school.
Henry was-- Henry was in the business.
And Henry was actually the sort of man that was handy to have when we would move.
And in fact, Henry was the moving force to-- to help out when we actually fled in 1938.
And Leo was at university, so the next brother it was Emmanuel, or [?
Manachaimendal ?] in Hebrew.
We used to call him Milos.
Milos ?] means lover in Czech.
He-- he-- well, he had a lovely nature, a very loving sort of person, boy.
He wasn't too loving with me when we were small because he-- I mean, he had a problem that I supplanted him because I became a baby.
I mean, he loved me because I was his dolly.
But when people played with the dolly, in preference to him, he had quite a problem with that.
He had another problem, and that was because we were swapping over to becoming Czech as a family, so the emphasis was to get us all into Czech schools.
So Emmanuel, when he had to start in his first class when he was six-- we used to have a-- a maid who was German, and she sort of brought him up, you know, next to his mother, obviously.
So until he was five, he had this Czech-- this German maid.
And he was most upset when my parents let her go in 1932.
And the new maid came, who was Czech, [?
Leeda. ?] And [?
Leeda ?] was like the second woman in my life because there was my mother and there was [?
Leeda. ?] And [?
Milos ?] never took to [?
Leeda ?] because he wanted his-- his old-- the maid from before who-- who loved him and who-- whom he loved.
And he spoke German.
He's the only one amongst us who wasn't immediately bilingual.
Well, my mother always spoke German or Yiddish.
She spoke German to us because German was the language of culture.
Yiddish was the language of folk type of-- when she wanted to say something pithy, she would say it in Yiddish.
So did my father, as a matter of fact.
Now, Yiddish was spoken a lot because of my mother's benevolent nature.
My mother was extraordinary in-- in her benevolence.
Well, good works.
For example, there was a street of pensioners in our hometown, and every Wednesday, we would-- my mother would cook a big bowl of soup-- potato soup, as a matter of fact.
I used to love potato soup.
And it's sort of brown with-- you know, you make roux and become-- the soup becomes brown and it tastes after the roux.
And it's full of onions and potatoes.
But she made this big pot of soup, and three or four loaves of bread.
Czech loaves of bread are round.
And I recall, when we were going to the street of the pensioners, that my brothers were carrying these loaves of bread under each arm.
And the loaf of bread was too big for my underarm, you know, because I was still quite small.
I mean, this is a very early memory in my hometown, how we walked this street.
The street was awful because it was all outside lavatories, very primitive.
There was no welfare in Czechoslovakia prewar, and they had very minute pensions.
The-- the hatters who were pensioned off from this factory all had-- you-- you know about Minamata disease?
Mercury poisoning?
Or hatters' disease-- you know, they were-- they were mad.
It's sort-- it's a sort of-- they shake and they can't find their center of gravity.
There's a lot of drunkenness in Czechoslovakia, anyway.
So in the street, the pension would be always taken by the men, and the men would use it for drink.
You know, on the day that it was paid, the men would get drunk, and then, the whole week, the family didn't have anything to live on, except on Wednesdays when-- when we came.
And my mother organized some other women, also, to do the same thing.
And I recall, for example, in the street, the children played hopscotch.
Hopscotch was a Czech game, just the same as in England.
And I wanted to join in, you know.
They were handing out soup.
It was all boring, but the kids wouldn't play with me.
And I was a nice child, you know.
I was-- I was not shy.
I couldn't understand why they wouldn't play with me.
OK.
[LAUGHS]
